questionText: The masses, $X$ grams, of bags of flour filled by a machine are modelled by $X \sim N(500, 8^2)$. Find the interval, symmetric about the mean, within which the masses of $95\%$ of the bags lie. Give the endpoints to $4$ significant figures.

steps.explanation: The masses are normal with mean $\mu = 500$ g and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$ g. We want a central interval, symmetric about $500$, holding $95\%$ of the bags.

steps.description: Split the outer 5% between the two tails
steps.explanation: If the central $95\%$ is symmetric about the mean, the remaining $5\%$ is shared equally between the two tails, leaving $0.025$ in each tail.

steps.description: Find the cumulative probability at the upper endpoint
steps.explanation: The upper endpoint $b$ has the central $95\%$ plus the lower tail's $2.5\%$ below it, so the area to its left is $0.975$.

steps.description: Use the inverse normal to find the z-value
steps.explanation: The standardised value with $97.5\%$ of the area below it is $1.960$. This is the well-known $z$ for a central $95\%$ interval.

steps.description: Write the endpoints in the form mu plus or minus z sigma
steps.explanation: By symmetry the two endpoints are the same distance from the mean, one at $z = +1.960$ and one at $z = -1.960$, i.e. $\mu \pm z\sigma$.

steps.description: Compute the half-width of the interval
steps.explanation: Multiplying the $z$-value by the standard deviation converts it back into grams: the endpoints lie $15.68$ g either side of the mean.
Subtracting the half-width from the mean gives the lower boundary of the interval.
Adding the half-width to the mean gives the upper boundary of the interval.

steps.description: State the interval to 4 significant figures
steps.explanation: So $95\%$ of bags have masses between $484.3$ g and $515.7$ g. The interval is centred on $500$ g, as expected for a symmetric central region.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $95\%$ of the bags have masses in the interval $484.3\text{ g} < X < 515.7\text{ g}$ (endpoints to 4 s.f.).
